label: Training, Tuning & Deployment — Part A
topic: Domain 1: Fundamentals of AI and ML

questions.stem: What are hyperparameters in machine learning?

options.text: External parameters set by data scientists before training that affect algorithm performance, such as the number of neural layers and nodes
Internal model weights that are learned during training
The input features of the training dataset
The output predictions made by the trained model

explanation.correct: Hyperparameters are external parameters set before training that affect performance (e.g., number of layers, nodes, learning rate). Their optimal values can only be determined by running multiple experiments.

distractors.B: Internal weights learned during training are model parameters, not hyperparameters.
distractors.C: Input features are the data columns. Hyperparameters configure the algorithm's behavior.
distractors.D: Outputs are predictions. Hyperparameters are algorithm settings configured before training.

questions.stem: What must you specify when creating a SageMaker training job?

options.text: The S3 bucket URL for training data, compute resources, output bucket for artifacts, and a Docker container image with the training algorithm
Only the model name — SageMaker handles everything else automatically
Only the hyperparameters — data and compute are auto-selected
Only the algorithm — SageMaker provides its own training data

explanation.correct: A SageMaker training job requires: S3 bucket with training data, compute resources, output bucket for model artifacts, and a Docker container image (from ECR) containing the training algorithm plus hyperparameters.

distractors.B: You must explicitly specify data location, compute, algorithm container, and output destination.
distractors.C: Data sources and compute must be specified. They are not auto-selected.
distractors.D: You must provide your own training data. SageMaker provides infrastructure, not data.

questions.stem: Why should data scientists run many training jobs in parallel with different algorithms and settings?

options.text: Running experiments with different algorithms and settings helps land on the best-performing solution, since optimal values can only be determined by testing multiple combinations
Running parallel jobs is required by AWS terms of service
Parallel jobs always produce identical results regardless of settings
Only one algorithm exists, so parallelism speeds up the single option

explanation.correct: Running experiments (multiple training jobs with different algorithms, hyperparameters, and configurations) is best practice because optimal values can only be found through experimentation.

distractors.B: Experimentation is best practice, not a requirement. It's recommended for finding optimal models.
distractors.C: Different settings produce different results. That's the purpose — finding which performs best.
distractors.D: Multiple algorithms exist for most problems. Experimenting across them identifies the best fit.

questions.stem: What does Amazon SageMaker Automatic Model Tuning (AMT) do?

options.text: It automatically finds the best hyperparameter values by running many training jobs and selecting the combination that maximizes a chosen metric
It automatically selects training data from the internet
It automatically deploys models to production without evaluation
It automatically writes the algorithm code for you

explanation.correct: SageMaker AMT (hyperparameter tuning) finds the best model version by running many training jobs with different hyperparameter values within ranges you specify, selecting the combination that maximizes your chosen metric.

distractors.B: AMT optimizes hyperparameters. You must provide your own training data.
distractors.C: AMT tunes during training. Deployment is a separate step requiring evaluation first.
distractors.D: AMT searches hyperparameter space. You still provide the algorithm/container.

questions.stem: What are the four SageMaker inference endpoint options?

options.text: Batch transform, asynchronous inference, serverless inference, and real-time inference
CPU inference, GPU inference, TPU inference, and FPGA inference
Standard, premium, enterprise, and free tier
Development, staging, production, and archive

explanation.correct: SageMaker supports four inference options: batch transform (offline, large datasets), asynchronous (queued, large payloads), serverless (Lambda-based, scales to zero), and real-time (persistent endpoint, low latency).

distractors.B: These are hardware types, not inference endpoint options. The four options are batch, async, serverless, and real-time.
distractors.C: These aren't SageMaker categories. Endpoint options are defined by workload pattern, not pricing tier.
distractors.D: These are environment stages. SageMaker's four inference options serve different workload patterns.

questions.stem: Which statements about SageMaker inference options are correct? (Select THREE)

options.text: Batch transform provides offline inference for large datasets without a persistent endpoint
Serverless inference uses Lambda and scales down to zero when there are no requests
Real-time inference provides a persistent managed REST API endpoint backed by chosen instance types
All four options require you to manage the underlying EC2 instances
Asynchronous inference cannot handle large payloads
Batch transform requires a persistent endpoint running 24/7

explanation.correct: Batch is offline without persistent endpoint. Serverless uses Lambda and scales to zero. Real-time provides persistent managed REST API on your chosen instances. All are fully managed with auto-scaling.

distractors.D: SageMaker manages endpoints fully. You don't manage EC2 instances directly for inference.
distractors.E: Asynchronous is specifically designed for large payloads with processing time. It queues requests.
distractors.F: Batch runs only during processing, then shuts down. It explicitly does not need a persistent endpoint.

questions.stem: Which AWS services can host Docker containers for ML model inference? (Select THREE)

options.text: Amazon ECS (Elastic Container Service)
Amazon EKS (Elastic Kubernetes Service)
AWS Lambda
Amazon S3
Amazon DynamoDB
AWS Glue Data Catalog

explanation.correct: Docker containers can run on ECS, EKS, Lambda, EC2, AWS Batch, and others. Containers are versatile and run on any compute with a container runtime.

distractors.D: S3 is object storage. It doesn't run Docker containers.
distractors.E: DynamoDB is a database. It doesn't execute container workloads.
distractors.F: The Data Catalog stores metadata. It doesn't run containers.

questions.stem: What does SageMaker do when you create a training job? (Select TWO)

options.text: Launches ML compute instances and uses training code with training dataset to train the model
Saves resulting model artifacts and outputs to the specified S3 bucket
Automatically deploys the model to a production endpoint
Sends the training data to the public internet for processing
Permanently stores the training data inside the model file

explanation.correct: SageMaker launches managed ML compute instances, uses training code + data to train the model, then saves model artifacts to the S3 output bucket you specified.

distractors.C: Training and deployment are separate steps. Training doesn't auto-deploy.
distractors.D: Training data stays within your AWS environment. It's not sent to the public internet.
distractors.E: Artifacts contain learned parameters, not raw training data.

questions.stem: What does Amazon SageMaker Experiments provide? (Select TWO)

options.text: Create, manage, analyze, and compare machine learning experiments across multiple training runs
Visual interface to browse experiments and compare runs on key performance metrics
Automatic code generation for ML algorithms
Physical GPU hardware provisioning
Data labeling for training datasets

explanation.correct: SageMaker Experiments lets you create/manage/analyze/compare experiments across training runs. It provides a visual interface to browse active and past experiments, compare on metrics, and identify best models.

distractors.C: Code generation is handled by other tools. Experiments track and compare training runs.
distractors.D: GPU provisioning is infrastructure. Experiments organize and compare training results.
distractors.E: Data labeling is Ground Truth's job. Experiments track training runs and results.

questions.stem: How does real-time inference work with a REST API on AWS? (Select TWO)

options.text: Clients send POST requests containing input data to an endpoint
The endpoint passes requests to compute resources running the model and returns the output in the response
Responses are delivered via email within 24 hours
Clients must download the model locally before sending requests
The model runs on the client's device, not the cloud

explanation.correct: Clients send POST requests with input data to an endpoint. The endpoint passes the request to compute (running the model), and the resulting output is sent back in the response in real time.

distractors.C: Real-time means immediate response, not 24-hour email delivery.
distractors.D: The model runs on cloud infrastructure. Clients send requests via API, not download models.
distractors.E: The model runs in the cloud (SageMaker, Lambda, EC2). Real-time API serves predictions remotely.

questions.scenario: A company runs ML inference once per month on the previous month's sales data (50 GB). They don't need instant results and want to minimize costs.
questions.stem: Which SageMaker inference option is most cost-effective for this use case?

options.text: Batch transform — offline inference for large datasets without a persistent endpoint; resources only run during processing
Real-time inference — persistent endpoint running 24/7
Serverless inference — Lambda-based for intermittent traffic
Asynchronous inference — queued requests with large payloads

explanation.correct: Batch transform is ideal for offline processing of large datasets (50 GB monthly). Computing resources only run during the batch job, making it the most cost-effective for infrequent bulk processing.

distractors.B: Real-time keeps resources running 24/7. For monthly batch processing, this wastes money on 29+ idle days.
distractors.C: Serverless handles real-time requests without provisioning. For 50 GB monthly batch, dedicated batch transform is more appropriate.
distractors.D: Async handles large payloads in a queue. Batch is better for scheduled offline processing of entire datasets.

questions.scenario: A startup's chatbot model has highly variable traffic — busy during business hours but zero requests overnight and weekends. They don't want to pay for idle resources.
questions.stem: Which SageMaker inference option best fits this traffic pattern?

options.text: Serverless inference — scales to zero when no requests arrive, so you only pay when functions are running
Real-time inference — persistent endpoint for consistent traffic
Batch transform — processes all requests at end of day
Asynchronous inference — queues all requests for later processing

explanation.correct: Serverless inference (Lambda-based) scales to zero with no requests, so you aren't charged during nights/weekends. It handles real-time requests when they arrive without over-provisioning.

distractors.B: Real-time keeps resources running 24/7. With zero overnight traffic, you'd pay for idle resources.
distractors.C: Batch is offline processing. A chatbot needs immediate responses, not end-of-day batch.
distractors.D: Async queues requests. A chatbot needs real-time interaction, not deferred processing.

questions.scenario: A data scientist is tuning a deep learning model. They're adjusting the number of hidden layers, learning rate, and batch size to find the best performance.
questions.stem: What are these adjustable settings called and how are optimal values found?

options.text: Hyperparameters — their optimal values can only be determined by running multiple experiments with different settings
Model parameters — they are automatically determined by the training data
Features — they are selected from the input data columns
Metrics — they measure model performance after training

explanation.correct: Number of layers, learning rate, and batch size are hyperparameters — external settings configured before training. Optimal values require running multiple experiments (or using AMT for automated search).

distractors.B: Model parameters (weights) are learned from data. Hyperparameters are set before training by humans.
distractors.C: Features are data attributes used as inputs. Hyperparameters configure the algorithm's behavior.
distractors.D: Metrics evaluate performance. Hyperparameters are the settings being tuned to optimize those metrics.

questions.scenario: An ML team wants to find the best hyperparameter values for their binary classification model. They want to maximize the area under the curve (AUC) metric without manually testing every combination.
questions.stem: Which SageMaker capability automates this hyperparameter search?

options.text: SageMaker Automatic Model Tuning (AMT) — runs many training jobs within specified hyperparameter ranges to maximize the chosen metric
SageMaker Model Monitor — tracks model drift in production
SageMaker Feature Store — manages training features
SageMaker Ground Truth — labels training data

explanation.correct: SageMaker AMT (hyperparameter tuning) runs multiple training jobs with different hyperparameter values within your specified ranges, choosing combinations that maximize your target metric (like AUC).

distractors.B: Model Monitor detects drift after deployment. AMT optimizes hyperparameters during training.
distractors.C: Feature Store manages features. AMT searches hyperparameter space to optimize performance.
distractors.D: Ground Truth labels data. AMT automates the search for optimal hyperparameters.

questions.scenario: During training, an ML algorithm updates weights iteratively. A junior engineer asks when this iterative process stops.
questions.stem: What determines when the iterative training process stops?

options.text: When a defined number of iterations have been run, or when the change in error is below a target value
When the data scientist manually clicks a stop button
When the model achieves exactly 100% accuracy
After exactly one iteration regardless of results

explanation.correct: Training stops either when a defined number of iterations (epochs) have been completed, or when the change in error falls below a target value (convergence). Both are stopping criteria.

distractors.B: While manual stopping is possible, training typically uses automated stopping criteria, not manual intervention.
distractors.C: 100% accuracy is rarely achievable and isn't the standard stopping criterion. Error convergence or max iterations are used.
distractors.D: One iteration is insufficient. Training requires many iterations for the algorithm to learn patterns in data.

questions.scenario: An ML architect is documenting the deployment options for their team. They need to match each option to its ideal use case.
questions.stem: Which correctly match SageMaker inference options to their ideal use cases? (Select TWO)

options.text: Asynchronous — ideal for large payloads with processing time, scales to zero between requests
Real-time — ideal for interactive low-latency responses backed by persistent managed endpoints
Batch — ideal for real-time chatbot interactions
Serverless — ideal for processing 100 GB datasets offline
Real-time — ideal when you can wait hours for results

explanation.correct: Asynchronous handles large payloads with queue-based processing, scaling to zero between requests. Real-time provides persistent low-latency REST API endpoints for interactive responses.

distractors.C: Batch is for offline processing. Chatbots need real-time or serverless for instant responses.
distractors.D: Serverless suits intermittent real-time requests. Large offline datasets suit batch transform.
distractors.E: Real-time is for instant responses. If you can wait, use batch or async instead.

questions.scenario: A DevOps engineer is preparing a model for deployment. They need to understand how models are packaged.
questions.stem: How are ML models typically packaged and deployed? (Select TWO)

options.text: Inference code and model artifacts are deployed as a Docker container
The container image is stored in Amazon ECR (Elastic Container Registry)
Models are deployed as spreadsheet files
Models are sent via email to production servers
Models are printed and scanned into the system

explanation.correct: Inference code and model artifacts are typically deployed as Docker containers. Container images are stored in Amazon ECR and referenced when creating endpoints.

distractors.C: Models are packaged as code + artifacts in containers, not spreadsheets.
distractors.D: Deployment uses container registries and APIs, not email delivery.
distractors.E: Models are digital artifacts deployed through container infrastructure.

questions.scenario: A web application needs to call an ML model for predictions. The architect is designing the integration.
questions.stem: Which AWS services can serve as the interface between a web client and an ML model? (Select TWO)

options.text: Amazon API Gateway — serves as the REST API interface with clients
AWS Lambda — runs model inference code when invoked by API requests
Amazon S3 — executes model code on stored objects
Amazon DynamoDB — processes inference requests
AWS IAM — handles model predictions

explanation.correct: API Gateway serves as the REST interface accepting client requests. Lambda can run the model code, processing requests and returning predictions. Together they provide a serverless inference architecture.

distractors.C: S3 stores data. It doesn't execute code or process inference requests.
distractors.D: DynamoDB is a database. It doesn't run model inference.
distractors.E: IAM manages permissions. It doesn't process model predictions.

questions.scenario: A team wants to deploy their model without managing infrastructure, patches, scaling, or network routing. They want SageMaker to handle everything.
questions.stem: What does SageMaker manage when you use its hosted inference?

options.text: SageMaker creates the endpoint, installs your model code, manages EC2 instances in auto-scaling groups, and handles all operational overhead
SageMaker only provides storage — you manage compute yourself
SageMaker only monitors — you must deploy and manage endpoints
SageMaker provides the algorithm — you manage all infrastructure

explanation.correct: SageMaker creates endpoints, installs your model, runs it on EC2 ML instances with auto-scaling, and manages all operational overhead (updates, patches, scaling, routing). You specify artifacts in S3 and container in ECR.

distractors.B: SageMaker fully manages compute for inference. You don't manage instances yourself.
distractors.C: SageMaker handles deployment end-to-end. Monitoring is one of many managed capabilities.
distractors.D: SageMaker manages all infrastructure. You bring the model artifacts and algorithm container.

questions.scenario: A team is unsure which instance type and configuration will perform best for their model. They want to test different options before choosing.
questions.stem: Which SageMaker tool helps test different configuration options to find the best one for your model?

options.text: SageMaker Inference Recommender — tests different configuration options with your model so you can pick the best one
SageMaker Ground Truth — labels data for training
SageMaker Clarify — detects bias in predictions
SageMaker Pipelines — orchestrates workflow steps

explanation.correct: SageMaker Inference Recommender tests different configuration options with your model, helping you select the optimal instance type and settings for deployment.

distractors.B: Ground Truth manages labeling. Inference Recommender tests deployment configurations.
distractors.C: Clarify detects bias. Inference Recommender evaluates compute configurations.
distractors.D: Pipelines orchestrate workflows. Inference Recommender specifically tests inference configurations.
